返回本批次

数学领域的人工智能革命已经到来

The AI revolution in math has arrived

www.quantamagazine.org 作者 sonabinu 分数 74 评论 40 2026/04/14 07:26
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 引言 转折点出现在 2025 年夏天。那年 7 月，一些人工智能模型解决了国际数学奥林匹克竞赛中六个问题中的五个，这是世界上一些最优秀高中生的年度挑战。然而，尽管数学家们感到震惊——很少有人预料到这些程序能这么快取得如此成果——令人印象深刻的结果并不一定意味着人工智能将在研究数学方面取得重要进展。毕竟，奥数题是具有已知答案的挑战性难题，而不是开放性问题。尽管如此，结果还是引起了人们的关注。那些认为人工智能模型太容易出错而无用的数学家们开始研究它们。那些早期采用者惊讶地发现，这些模型不仅擅长解谜，而且可以帮助开辟真正的新天地。很快，数学家们开始使用人工智能来发现和证明新结果，在一天内完成了以前需要数周或数月才能完成的工作。 “2025 年是人工智能真正开始对许多不同任务发挥作用的一年，”加州大学洛杉矶分校著名数学家陶哲轩 (Terence Tao) 说。虽然没有一项新成果是举世瞩目的突破，但其中一些成果可与专业数学期刊上发表的发现相媲美。在某些情况下，算法会提出一个猜想，证明它，并以最少的人为干预来验证证明。在其他情况下，与 ChatGPT、Claude 或 Gemini 等大型语言模型的广泛聊天会产生新颖的证明策略。 “这个人有一把铲子。这个人有一把镐。我们一起可以挖一条隧道，”陶说。 “很多人都会把东西扔到墙上，看看什么能粘住。”尽管陶可能是人工智能在数学中的应用的最杰出的倡导者，但其他人也同意这一点。多伦多大学的丹尼尔·利特 (Daniel Litt) 表示，即使通过解决简单的问题，人工智能也“正在改变数学的计算方式”。很快，“它看起来和感觉起来都将与传统的数学计算方式完全不同，”陶说。在数学家一次研究一个问题之前，“使用这些工具，您可以一次解决数千个问题并开始进行统计研究。”尽管与我交谈的人中没有人认为人工智能会取代数学家，但陶补充道，“我们必须做出很多制度变革和文化变革。”
  2. 这些变化将受到争议，无论是在数学领域，还是在其他学科领域，都在与人工智能的影响作斗争。高等研究院的阿克谢·文卡特什 (Akshay Venkatesh) 表示，随着人工智能模型成为一种强大的新工具，它们可能会导致数学家失去数学理解的直接经验。和陶一样，文卡特什也是数学界最负盛名的奖项菲尔兹奖的获得者。两人都认为人工智能的影响将是巨大的，但 Venkatesh 对此更加谨慎：“我们的文化中有一些有价值的东西，我们应该努力保留，”他说。一些数学家现在离开学术界，前往 OpenAI 和 Google 等大型科技公司工作，或者加入 Harmonic、Logical Intelligence、Axiom Math 和 Math Inc 等专注于数学的人工智能初创公司。卡内基梅隆大学数学计算机辅助推理。到 2026 年初，对人工智能力量的震惊已经变成了更多的奇迹。 2 月份，一项名为“First Proof”的挑战赛为参赛者提供了一周的时间，让他们的人工智能模型解决各个数学领域的 10 个研究级问题。数学家选择了这些问题，以便它们不太可能出现在算法的训练数据中。通过不同程度的自主权，这些模型成功解决了一半以上的问题。如果说奥林匹克竞赛的结果代表了人工智能进入雄心勃勃的大学数学课程的那一刻，那么第一个证明的结果可以说是他们完成研究生院的那一刻。利特在分析结果的博客文章中写道：“这项技术很可能比计算机更强大。”创意进化虽然 2025 年夏天标志着人工智能能力的拐点，但它并不是凭空出现的。谷歌 DeepMind 科学副总裁 Pushmeet Kohli 表示，DeepMind 自 2018 年以来一直在尝试用人工智能解决数学问题。现供职于 Axiom 的 François Charton 早在 2019 年就开始尝试使用机器学习来解决数学问题。但在早些年，这还是一个小众领域。起初，查顿和其他一些人使用人工智能来解决已知解决方案的问题，只是为了看看他们是否能让新技术发挥作用。到2024年，他们开始奋进。他们寻找有丰富数据可供分析的问题，然后使用人工智能构建具有可量化属性的数学对象，例如可以适合网格而不形成等腰三角形的点的最佳排列。
  3. 2025 年 1 月，布朗大学的 Tai 和 Javier Gómez-Serrano 开始与 DeepMind 的两位数学家 Adam Wagner 和 Bogdan Georgiev 合作开发一个名为 AlphaEvolve 的人工智能系统。 AlphaEvolve 的工作原理是使用 Gemini 用 Python 代码编写可能长达数百行的程序。然后，它使用所谓的遗传算法“进化”这些程序，试图找到数学问题的最佳解决方案。四位数学家在几个月的时间里每天或每两天都使用 AlphaEvolve 来解决新问题。在这样做的同时，他们还学会了如何改进给 AlphaEvolve 的提示。一个关键要点：该模型似乎受益于鼓励。戈麦斯-塞拉诺说，“当我们对法学硕士进行一些积极的强化时”效果更好。 “就像说‘你能做到’——这似乎有帮助。这很有趣。我们不知道为什么。”
  4. 到 5 月底，该团队已尝试 AlphaEvolve 解决多个数学领域的 67 个不同问题。 AlphaEvolve 对其中 23 个最知名的解决方案进行了小幅改进。在 67 个中的 36 个中，它的表现与已经完成的一样好，而在剩下的少数几个中，它无法与最著名的结果相匹配。数学家们在 2025 年 11 月发表的论文《大规模数学探索与发现》中分享了他们的发现。戈麦斯-塞拉诺指出，他们的任何一项结果都可能是由某一特定领域的专家在该领域工作了几个月后获得的。但他说，虽然我们不是这些领域的专家，但“我们能够在一两天内获得类似的结果”。正如陶所说，当前的人工智能模型“非常擅长从一大堆问题中寻找容易实现的目标。这是乏味且吃力不讨好的事情，不是人类愿意做的事情。”他警告说，模型正在“在大量未报告的失​​败中取得零星的成功”。但成功是显着的。戈麦斯-塞拉诺估计，他现在大约三分之二的时间都在使用人工智能。他说，“它已经达到了有用和可用的地步。这是我们研究数学的新方法的开始。”错误的身份在前几年，人工智能的额外力量似乎来自于它能够重新浮现隐藏在模糊参考文献中长期被遗忘的证据。加州大学洛杉矶分校 (UCLA) 的伊戈尔·帕克 (Igor Pak) 指出，ChatGPT 目前“在找到正确的参考文献、正确的文献、找到 Google Scholar（在语义上不起作用）无法找到的联系方面表现出色。”瑞士苏黎世联邦理工学院的约翰内斯·施密特 (Johannes Schmitt) 表示，到了 2025 年，事情发生了变化。 “与法学硕士交谈开始变得有用，不是因为他们会给你完整的答案，”他说，而是因为“他们成为了很好的对话伙伴。”
  5. 与他交谈过的法学硕士不可避免地犯了很多错误，导致一些数学家彻底驳回了他们。他说，许多研究人员认为，如果“它所说的一切都是错误的，我就不会和它说话。”但其他人——他把自己归入这个阵营——对“与这个胡言乱语的模型交谈的痛苦有更高的容忍度。他们说，我仍然可以从这次谈话中得到一些东西；即使不是每个想法都是好的，我也可以忽略坏的想法并接受好的想法。”施密特指出，这些错误都是奇怪的：一个受过数学训练的人几乎不可能在犯如此多的基本错误的同时，还能成功地提出微妙的、原创的和正确的想法。加州大学洛杉矶分校 (UCLA) 的欧内斯特·柳 (Ernest Ryu) 主要研究应用数学的一个分支——优化理论，在奥赛成绩公布后，他也开始更加关注法学硕士。 AlphaEvolve 试图优化特定数量，而 Ryu 想要证明优化算法工作的条件。 2025年夏天，他注意到法学硕士的数学能力有了显着提高。他开始使用它们来帮助准备讲义，主要是为了填补他对特定证明细节的记忆空白。他说，有时“它会发现我的推理中的错误，有时是大错误，有时是小错误。有时它会发现比我笔记中的证明更简单的证明。”

Original Summary

  1. Introduction The tipping point came in the summer of 2025. That July, several artificial intelligence models solved five out of six problems at the International Mathematical Olympiad, an annual challenge for some of the world’s best high school students. But while mathematicians were shocked — few had expected the programs to get that good that quickly — the impressive results didn’t necessarily mean that AI would make important strides in research math. After all, Olympiad problems are challenging puzzles with known answers, not open questions. Nevertheless, the results made people pay attention. Mathematicians who had dismissed AI models as too error-prone to be useful started playing around with them. Those early adopters found, to their surprise, not only that the models were good at puzzles, but that they could help break genuinely new ground. Soon, mathematicians were using AI to discover and prove new results, accomplishing in a day what would have once taken them weeks or months. “2025 was the year when AI really started being useful for many different tasks,” said Terence Tao, a prominent mathematician at the University of California, Los Angeles. While no single new result is a world-beating breakthrough, some of them are on par with discoveries published in professional mathematical journals. In some cases, algorithms formulate a conjecture, prove it, and verify the proof with minimal human intervention. In others, extensive chats with large language models such as ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini lead to novel proof strategies. “This guy’s got a shovel. This guy’s got a pickax. Together we can bore a tunnel,” Tao said. There’s “a lot of throwing things at the wall to see what sticks.” Though Tao is perhaps the most prominent exponent of AI’s utility in mathematics, others agree. Even by solving easy problems, said Daniel Litt of the University of Toronto, AI “is changing how mathematics is done.” Soon, “it will look and feel altogether different from the way mathematics was traditionally done,” Tao said. Where before mathematicians studied one problem at a time, “with these tools you can solve thousands of problems at once and start doing statistical studies.” Though nobody I spoke with thinks AI will replace mathematicians, Tao added that “there are a lot of institutional changes, cultural changes, we will have to make.”
  2. Those changes will be contested, in math as in other academic disciplines wrestling with AI’s impact. As AI models become a powerful new tool, they risk causing mathematicians to lose direct experience with mathematical understanding, said Akshay Venkatesh of the Institute for Advanced Study. Like Tao, Venkatesh is a recipient of the Fields Medal, math’s most prestigious prize. Both agree that AI’s impact will be significant, but Venkatesh is more cautious about it: “There are valuable things in our culture which we should try to keep,” he said. Some mathematicians are now leaving academia to work at big tech firms, like OpenAI and Google, or to join math-focused AI startups such as Harmonic, Logical Intelligence, Axiom Math, and Math Inc. “One reason there is so much interest in AI for mathematics in the corporate world is that people are recognizing that the key to general intelligence is combining the insights you get from machine learning and the precision you get from mathematics,” said Jeremy Avigad, the director of the Institute for Computer-Aided Reasoning in Mathematics at Carnegie Mellon University. By the start of 2026, shock at the power of AI had turned into something more like wonder. A February challenge called First Proof gave entrants a week to have their AI models solve 10 research-level questions in various areas of math. Mathematicians had chosen the questions so that they were unlikely to have appeared in the algorithms’ training data. With varying levels of autonomy, the models succeeded in solving over half the problems. If the Olympiad results represented the moment AI entered an ambitious college math program, the First Proof results were arguably the moment they finished graduate school. In a blog post analyzing the results, Litt wrote: “It’s very likely that this technology is bigger than the computer.” Creative Evolution Though the summer of 2025 marked an inflection point in the capabilities of AI, it didn’t come out of nowhere. Pushmeet Kohli, Google DeepMind’s vice president of science, said DeepMind has been trying to solve math problems with AI since 2018. François Charton, now at Axiom, first started trying to use machine learning to solve mathematical problems back in 2019. But in those early years, it was a niche area. At first, Charton and a handful of others used AI to solve problems whose solutions were already known, just to see if they could get the new techniques to work. By 2024, they were beginning to forge ahead. They looked for problems where there was a rich set of data to analyze, and then used AI to construct mathematical objects with quantifiable properties — like optimal arrangements of points that could fit on a grid without forming an isosceles triangle.
  3. In January 2025, Tao and Javier Gómez-Serrano of Brown University began working with two mathematicians at DeepMind, Adam Wagner and Bogdan Georgiev, on an AI system called AlphaEvolve. AlphaEvolve works by using Gemini to write programs in Python code that might be hundreds of lines long. It then “evolves” these programs using so-called genetic algorithms to attempt to find optimal solutions to math problems. The four mathematicians used AlphaEvolve on a new problem every day or two for a few months. As they did so, they also learned how to improve the prompts they gave AlphaEvolve. One key takeaway: The model seemed to benefit from encouragement. It worked better “when we were prompting with some positive reinforcement to the LLM,” Gómez-Serrano said. “Like saying ‘You can do this’ — this seemed to help. This is interesting. We don’t know why.”
  4. By late May, the team had tried AlphaEvolve on 67 different problems in several areas of mathematics. On 23 of them, AlphaEvolve improved, in a small way, on the best known solutions. On 36 of the 67, it did as well as what had already been done, and on the remaining handful, it couldn’t match the best known result. The mathematicians shared their findings in a November 2025 paper, “Mathematical Exploration and Discovery at Scale.” Gómez-Serrano noted that any one of their results might have been obtained by an expert in a given area who worked at it for a few months. But without being experts in many of these fields, “we were able to obtain comparable results in the span of a day or two,” he said. As Tao put it, current AI models are “very good at scouring big lists of problems for low-hanging fruit. It’s tedious and thankless and not something humans want to do.” He cautioned that models are achieving “scattered successes among a big sea of unreported failures.” But the successes are notable. Gómez-Serrano estimates that he now spends about two-thirds of his time using AI. It is, he said, “getting to the point where it is useful and usable. This is the beginning of the new way we will do mathematics.” Mistaken Identities In previous years, AI’s extra power seemed to come from its ability to resurface long-forgotten proofs buried in obscure references. Igor Pak of UCLA noted that ChatGPT is currently “fantastic in finding the right references, right literature, finding connections that Google Scholar — which doesn’t work semantically — can’t.” Then, over the course of 2025, said Johannes Schmitt of the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Zurich, something shifted. “It started becoming useful to talk to LLMs, not because they would give you the full answer,” he said, but because “they became good conversation partners.”
  5. The LLMs he spoke with inevitably made lots of mistakes, leading some mathematicians to dismiss them outright. Many researchers, he said, decide that if “everything it says is kind of wrong, I will just not talk to it.” But others — he puts himself in this camp — have a higher tolerance for “the pain of talking to this bullshitting model. They say, I can still get something out of this conversation; even if not every idea is good, I can ignore the bad ones and take the good ones.” And the mistakes, Schmitt noted, are weird ones: There is virtually no way that a person with any training in mathematics would make such a plethora of basic errors while also succeeding in coming up with subtle, original, and correct ideas. UCLA’s Ernest Ryu, who works largely in a branch of applied math called optimization theory, also started paying more attention to LLMs after the Olympiad results. Whereas AlphaEvolve was trying to optimize particular quantities, Ryu wanted to prove things about the conditions under which optimization algorithms work. In the summer of 2025, he noticed that LLMs’ mathematical capabilities had dramatically improved. He started using them to help prepare lecture notes, mostly to fill in gaps in his memory of the details of a particular proof. At times, he said, “it would find an error in my reasoning, sometimes major, sometimes minor. Sometimes it would find a simpler proof than I had in my notes.”

评论（默认中文）

bgirard 2026/04/14 12:24
上周我和我的数学校友朋友聚在一起。我们喝了一些啤酒，用语音模式 ChatGPT 聊天，玩弄 Collat​​z 猜想，并向编码代理发送了一些提示以构建可视化和模拟。当我们不断提出想法并且模型可以探索它们时，指导这些代理非常有趣。

我认为，有了正确的问题和正确的代理循环，我很清楚改进将会加快。

Last week I got together with my math alumni friend. We cracked some beers, we chatted with voice mode ChatGPT and toyed around with Collatz Conjecture and we sent some prompt to a coding agent to build visualizations and simulation. It was a lot of fun directing these agents while we bounced off ideas and the models could explore them.

I think with the right problem and the right agentic loop it’s clear to me improvements will speed up.

我认为语音模式使用较弱的模型，仅供参考相对于 SOTA
I think voice mode uses weaker models, just an FYI relative to the SOTA

scrollop 2026/04/14 13:41

Definitely, seems like gpt 3
在 HN 中查看
dogscatstrees 2026/04/14 11:12
> 在这样做的同时，他们还学会了如何改进给 AlphaEvolve 的提示。一个关键要点：该模型似乎受益于鼓励。戈麦斯-塞拉诺说，“当我们对法学硕士进行一些积极的强化时”效果更好。 “就像说‘你能做到’——这似乎有帮助。这很有趣。我们不知道为什么。”

世界上四位顶尖逻辑人都承认这一点。这是令人兴奋的，我们不知道为什么。

> As they did so, they also learned how to improve the prompts they gave AlphaEvolve. One key takeaway: The model seemed to benefit from encouragement. It worked better “when we were prompting with some positive reinforcement to the LLM,” Gómez-Serrano said. “Like saying ‘You can do this’ — this seemed to help. This is interesting. We don’t know why.”

Four top logical people in the world are acknowledging this. It is mind-blowing and we don't know why.

在 HN 中查看
dataviz1000 2026/04/14 11:38
我知道为什么。

有几个人在十四行诗中遇到了问题，他们在解决一个无法解决的问题时耗尽了所有的学分。 Opus 解决了这个问题——它有一个置信阈值，低于该阈值它会退出任务而不是苦苦挣扎。

“我上周花了约 100 美元测试了两者的乘法。Sonnet 在 37 位 × 37 位 (~10³⁷) 上永远不会停止 — 15 分钟以上，211KB 的输出，当我停止它时仍在积极分解数字。Opus 将真正尝试最多约 50 位数字（真正尝试时为 112K 令牌），开始怀疑大约 55 位数字，并通过 80 位数字 × 80 位数字9 秒内 330 个代币投降，答案空洞。” -- Opus，帮助我处理数据

“我认为这不值得尝试”启发式就是区别。 Sonnet 没有它，或者它设置得更高。为了让 Opus 和其他一些模型能够解决它认为不值得尝试的更困难的问题，需要提高置信水平。

这周我会写完这篇文章。我现在正在制作华丽的数据视觉动画来阐明这一点。

I know why.

Several people had problems with Sonnet burning through all their credits grinding on a problem it can't solve. Opus fixes this — it has a confidence threshold below which it exits the task instead of grinding.

"I spent ~$100 last week testing both against multiplication. Sonnet at 37-digit × 37-digit (~10³⁷) never quits — 15+ minutes, 211KB of output, still actively decomposing numbers when I stopped it. Opus will genuinely attempt up to ~50 digits (112K tokens on a real try), starts doubting around 55 digits, and by 80-digit × 80-digit surrenders in 330 tokens / 9 seconds with an empty answer." -- Opus, helping me with the data

The "I don't think this is worth attempting" heuristic is the difference. Sonnet doesn't have it, or has it set much higher. In order to get Opus and some other models to work on harder problems that it assumes it is not worth attempting, it requires an increase of confidence level.

I'll finish writing this up this week. I'm making flashy data visual animations to make the point right now.

在 HN 中查看
zarzavat 2026/04/14 11:41
这对我来说很有意义。

最初，LLM 会陷入无限循环中，永远生成代币。这很糟糕，所以我们训练他们在完成答案后强烈倾向于停下来。

然而，训练模型停止也给了他们“懒惰”，因为他们可能更喜欢较短的答案，而不是实际回答用户问题的曲折答案。

数学之所以不同寻常，是因为它有外部的真理来源（证明助手），也因为它需要长期曲折的思考，探索许多死胡同。这与模型的训练目的存在冲突。因此，给他们一些鼓励可以让他们处于正确的状态，真正尝试解决问题。

It makes sense to me.

Originally LLMs would get stuck in infinite loops generating tokens forever. This is bad, so we trained them to strongly prefer to stop once they reached the end of their answer.

However, training models to stop also gave them "laziness", because they might prefer a shorter answer over a meandering answer that actually answered the user's question.

Mathematics is unusual because it has an external source of truth (the proof assistant), and also because it requires long meandering thinking that explores many dead ends. This is in tension with what models have been trained to do. So giving them some encouragement keeps them in the right state to actually attempt to solve the problem.

在 HN 中查看
brookst 2026/04/14 11:24
我们知道为什么它对人类有效吗？

模型根据人类输出进行训练。对我来说，遵循令人鼓舞的模式的输入会产生更好的结果输出，这并不奇怪。许多培训材料都反映了这一点。

Do we know why it works for humans?

Models are trained on human outputs. It’s not super surprising to me that inputs following encouraging patterns product better results outputs; much of the training material reflects that.

latentsea 2026/04/14 11:33
> 我们知道为什么它对人类有效吗？

试着弄清楚。你可以做到的。

> Do we know why it works for humans?

Try to figure it out. You can do it.

在 HN 中查看
gxs 2026/04/14 11:29
如果我不得不打赌一个懒惰的、纸上谈兵的猜测，我认为这会迫使它更努力/更长时间地思考

答案可能比我们想象的更简单，例如“用户认为我可以做到这一点，所以我最好确保我没有错过任何东西”

If I had to wager a lazy, armchair guess, I think it forces it to think harder/longer

The answer is probably more straightforward than we think, e.g. “the user thinks I can do this so I better make sure I didn’t miss anything”

在 HN 中查看
CivBase 2026/04/14 11:34
这看起来很明显，不是吗？

这是培训材料上的模式匹配。几乎可以肯定，培训材料中的积极性和成功之间存在重叠。积极的提示会导致模式匹配偏向积极性，从而获得更成功的材料。

This seems pretty obvious, no?

It's pattern matching on training material. There is almost certainly an overlap between positivity and success in the training material. Positive prompts cause the pattern matching to weight towards positivity and therefor more successful material.

在 HN 中查看
lamasery 2026/04/14 11:59
训练或系统提示已将概率推向倾向于更快选择“停止”的空间。您需要拖动概率权重，直到它们不太可能这么快达到“停止”状态。

好的语言通常会为他们看到的任何模型做到这一点，并且也是人们可能尝试的东西。也许很多很多无意义的标记组合会效果更好，但谁会尝试在提示的末尾贴上“老年绿色巨型长颈鹿”来看看是否有帮助呢？

积极或消极的语言也可能会妨碍将概率从正确的主题中拉出来，因为事情太笼统了。如果主题是催化转换器，由于某种原因，上述建议可能只会非常有效，否则会推动生成有关长颈鹿的代币。您如何发现数十或数千个更有效但有时只是无意义的令牌组合？你需要自动化和大量的强力，或者一些更好的方法来分析法学硕士的数据库。

The training or system prompts have shoved the probabilities toward a space that tends to select “halt” sooner. You need to drag the probability weights around until they are less likely to reach “halt” so soon.

Nice language often sorta does this for whatever model(s) they looked at, and is also something people are likely to try. Probably lots and lots of nonsense token combos would work even better, but who’s gonna try sticking “gerontocratic green giant giraffes” on the end of their prompts to see if it helps?

Positive or negative language likely also prevents pulling the probabilities away from the correct topic, being so generic a thing. The above suggestion might only be ultra-effective if the topic is catalytic converters, for some reason, and push the thing into generating tokens about giraffes otherwise. How would you ever discover the dozens or thousands of more-effective but only-sometimes nonsense token combos? You’d need automation and a lot of brute force, or some better way to analyze the LLM’s database.

在 HN 中查看
4ajsH17 2026/04/14 13:41
谁资助了 Quantamamagazine.org？为什么引用的总是道？

这是即将到来的首次公开募股的广告，因为人工智能到处都会失败。包括数学领域，那里没有严肃的结果，只有那些在人工智能加入潮流之前就已经喜欢修补精益的人。

也许数学家们嫉妒地看着软件工程师，他们只能通过一直修补新工具而没有取得真正的进步而生存，并认为：我们也可以做到这一点！

Who funds quantamagazine.org? Why is it always Tao who is cited?

This is an ad for the upcoming IPOs because AI fails everywhere. Including in math, where there are no serious results and just the people who already loved tinkering with Lean before AI jump on the bandwagon.

Maybe mathematicians have looked jealously at software engineers who can survive by just tinkering with new tools all the time without making real progress and think: We can do this, too!

在 HN 中查看
Wissenschafter 2026/04/14 13:54
更多新卢德派的废话。
More neo-luddite nonsense.
在 HN 中查看
sm0ss117 2026/04/14 12:11
数学似乎是人工智能实现荒谬结果的理想选择。它是一种纯粹的抽象语法，具有真正的自动验证性。甚至 SWE 也有与真实物理事物交互的需求。在数学中，不需要外部反馈，您仅受代币生成的速率和质量的限制。
Mathematics seems like the ideal candidate for AIs to achieve absurd results. It's a purely abstract grammar with true auto-verifiability. Even SWE has the requirement of interacting with real physical things. In math there's no external feedback required, you're solely bounded by the rate and quality of token generation.
在 HN 中查看
drivebyhooting 2026/04/14 12:33
这至少在两个账户上没有达到目标： 1. 没有人类理解的证明对数学家来说价值较低 2. 至少目前来说，有趣程度取决于人的判断。它是主观的并且不可验证。
This misses the mark on at least two accounts: 1. Proofs without human understanding have less value for mathematicians 2. At least for now, interestingness depends on human judgment. It is subjective and not as verifiable.
在 HN 中查看
dyauspitr 2026/04/14 12:48
每一位新出现的数学家并不知道他之前的一切。他需要学习他的前辈们学过的所有数学知识。我不明白法学硕士提出的这些证明会如何改变这一点。
Every new mathematician that comes along doesn’t know everything that has come before him. He needs to go learn all the math that his predecessors did. I don’t see how an LLM coming up with these proofs changes that.
在 HN 中查看
streb-lo 2026/04/14 13:17
因为问题空间基本上是无限的。 如果一个人正在研究一个问题，那么可能至少有一个人会对这个问题感兴趣。 随机遍历问题空间 可能 很有趣，但我不知道这个信号对其他人类来说会如何。
Because the problem space is basically infinite. If a person is working on a problem, its probably interesting to at least one person. Randomly walking through the problem space might be interesting, but I don't know how the signal will fare against other humans.
在 HN 中查看
meroes 2026/04/14 13:38
语法似乎你在专门谈论法学硕士。那么，数独不就是数学吗？我上次检查时，法学硕士在数独方面很糟糕。当被告知不要编写求解器时，它的第一个推论就是错误的。
Grammar seems like you’re talking about LLMs specifically. Well, isn’t Sudoku just math? LLMs suck at Sudoku last I checked. When told not to code a solver, its very first deduction was wrong.
在 HN 中查看
claysmithr 2026/04/14 10:19
我想知道人工智能什么时候能够辨别时间的流逝
I wonder when AI will be able to discern the passage of time
在 HN 中查看
1970-01-01 2026/04/14 10:38
它已经在提示块中计时了。它知道时间是线性的，知道刚刚发生了什么，之前发生了什么，以及之前发生了什么。
It already does time in prompt-blocks. It knows time is linear and what just happened, what happened before that, and what happened before that.
在 HN 中查看
claysmithr 2026/04/14 10:47
当我尝试使用它作为人工智能首席执行官和生活教练时，它永远无法辨别时间的流逝，我已经做了什么，需要做什么。它只是一遍又一遍地说同样的事情，我已经做过的事情。那它有点停留在它接受训练的时代。如果它像人类一样感觉到时间流逝，也许它会有意识？

然而，没有时间观念会让你很难计划任何事情。我用的是双子座专业版。

When I tried to use it as an AI CEO and Life Coach, it never was able to discern time passing, what I've already done, what needed to be done. It just said the same stuff over and over, stuff I've already done. That and it's kind of stuck in the era it was trained in. If it felt time passing like a human maybe it would be conscious?

Nevertheless not having a sense of time makes it really bad at planning anything. I used Gemini Pro.

在 HN 中查看
Buttons840 2026/04/14 11:29
你不能在每个提示中给出时间吗？那行得通吗？

我已经多次看到这一点，所以我想也许它比这更复杂？

Can't you just give it the time in each prompt? Would that work?

I've seen this mentioned a few times though, so I think maybe it's more complicated than this?

在 HN 中查看
maplethorpe 2026/04/14 10:33

https://tech.yahoo.com/ai/chatgpt/articles/chatgpt-fails-mis...

Altman has estimated one year until ChatGPT is capable of measuring time passed.

https://tech.yahoo.com/ai/chatgpt/articles/chatgpt-fails-mis...

ambicapter 2026/04/14 10:50

Sounds like Musk setting deadlines for Mars landings.

keyle 2026/04/14 13:41

It's so hard to predict you know, these planets keep moving...

VladVladikoff 2026/04/14 10:57

Can’t tell if you are being sarcastic but Altman’s whole job is to make bullshit near future predictions about rapid development of AI in the public.
在 HN 中查看
random__duck 2026/04/14 11:04
感谢您陈述了显而易见的事情，出于某种原因我们需要重复这一点。 ^^;
Thankyou for stating the obvious, for some reason we need to repeat this. ^^;
在 HN 中查看
viccis 2026/04/14 12:52
没有必要“估计”它。 “时间”并不是生成分布的训练和采样中内置的东西。他可能还告诉你你的朴素贝叶斯电子邮件过滤器会测量过去的时间。
There's no need to "estimate" it. "Time" is not something built into training and sampling a generative distribution. He might as well have told you your Naive Bayes email filters will measure time passed.
在 HN 中查看
yabutlivnWoods 2026/04/14 12:27
我们可以用数学来定义戴森球。

我们无法建造一个。

人工智能输出公理上有效的语法不会那么有用。可以使用 for 循环生成所有公理正确的数学，直到机器 OOM

物理不是数学，数学也不是物理。

We can define a Dyson Sphere in math.

We cannot build one.

AI outputting axiomatically valid syntax isn't going to be all that useful. It's possible to generate all axiomatically correct math with a for loop until the machine OOMs

Physics is not math and math is not physics.

在 HN 中查看
djsjajah 2026/04/14 12:44
你刚刚未通过图灵测试。
You just failed the Turing test.
在 HN 中查看
keyle 2026/04/14 13:38
也许他以 88.2% 通过图灵测试，比竞争对手高出 1.8%。
Maybe he passed the Turing test with 88.2% which is 1.8% higher than the competition.
在 HN 中查看
goatlover 2026/04/14 13:20
图灵测试刚刚让你失败了。我会说得更好，物理不是现实，它是利用数学的现实模型。
The Turing test just failed you. I'll go one better, physics isn't reality, it's a model of reality utilizing math.
在 HN 中查看
norejisace 2026/04/14 11:26
有趣的发展。感觉人工智能在符号推理方面变得越来越好，而不仅仅是模式识别。
Interesting development. It feels like AI is getting much better at symbolic reasoning, not just pattern recognition.
在 HN 中查看
viccis 2026/04/14 13:20
人工智能解决纯数学问题的目的是什么？ AI关心数学吗？
What is the telos for AI chewing around the edges of pure math problems? Does AI care about math?
在 HN 中查看
themafia 2026/04/14 10:56
数学研究有几个高价值奖项。 当“AI”赢得其中之一时请告诉我。 否则：

> 当 Ryu 询问 ChatGPT 时，“它一直给我错误的证明”，[...] 他会检查答案，保留正确的部分，然后将它们反馈到模型中

所以你有一个由真正的领域专家操作的会话计算器。

> 通过 ChatGPT，我感觉自己很快就掌握了很多知识

没有办法将这种感觉转化为任何实际价值的衡量标准，而且我们碰巧知道，领域专家在自己的领域之外时非常容易被愚弄。

There are several high value prizes for mathematical research. Let me know when an "AI" has earned one of them. Otherwise:

> When Ryu asked ChatGPT, “it kept giving me incorrect proofs,” [...] he would check its answers, keep the correct parts, and feed them back into the model

So you had a conversational calculator being operated by an actual domain expert.

> With ChatGPT, I felt like I was covering a lot of ground very rapidly

There's no way to convert that feeling into a measurement of any actual value and we happen to know that domain experts are surprisingly easy to fool when outside of their own domains.

在 HN 中查看
gxs 2026/04/14 11:31
哇，这是你的外卖吗？

> “2025 年是人工智能真正开始对许多不同任务发挥作用的一年，”陶哲轩说道

我想我会冒险同意陶哲轩的观点，我认为这家伙在数学界很有名，或者什么的

Wow that was your takeaway?

> “2025 was the year when AI really started being useful for many different tasks,” said Terence Tao

I think I’ll go out on a limb and agree with Terrence Tao, I think the dude is well known in the math community, or something

在 HN 中查看
themafia 2026/04/14 12:19
> 冒险并同意陶哲轩的观点

AI是他的专长吗？

> 我认为这家伙在数学界或其他领域很有名

我相信这就是所谓的“诉诸权威”。 这就是为什么我没有不同意他的观点，而是提出了一个更有说服力的终点，可以用来确定文章标题所暗示的事实。

> go out on a limb and agree with Terrence Tao

Is AI his specialty?

> I think the dude is well known in the math community, or something

I believe this is called "appeal to authority." Which is why, instead of disagreeing with him, I suggested a more cogent endpoint that could be used to establish the facts the article's title suggests.

p1dda 2026/04/14 11:48

noobermin 2026/04/14 11:34
